name: San Diego Unified School District
districtFact: Founded in 1854 — among the oldest school districts in California — San Diego Unified is the second-largest district in the state and serves one of the most geographically distinctive student populations in the country: a city defined by the largest concentration of active-duty military personnel in the United States and a cross-border community with deep ties to Tijuana and Baja California.

about.title: About San Diego Unified School District
about.content: San Diego Unified School District (SDUSD) is the second-largest school district in California, serving approximately 100,000 students across more than 225 schools. Founded in 1854, SDUSD is one of the oldest public school systems in California and operates a wide range of academic programs across neighborhood schools, magnet campuses, and school choice pathways. The district offers International Baccalaureate (IB) programs at select middle and high school campuses, Dual Language Immersion programs in Spanish and Mandarin, GATE and gifted pathways, and Career Technical Education (CTE) courses aligned with San Diego's major employment sectors — biotechnology, defense, healthcare, and hospitality. Advanced Placement (AP) courses are widely available at district high schools. SDUSD operates on a traditional school calendar, typically beginning in late August and ending in late May or early June. Families new to California should also note the 'Minimum Day' schedule used throughout the year — students dismiss 2–3 hours earlier on these days for professional development, parent conferences, and assessment periods. Minimum Day dates are published at the start of each school year on the SDUSD calendar.

about.title: School Choice and Magnet Programs
about.content: SDUSD offers structured school choice through magnet programs and specialized schools, allowing families to apply to schools outside their neighborhood attendance zone. Magnet programs include Dual Language Immersion, performing arts, STEM, and gifted/GATE pathways — each with its own enrollment window and lottery process when applications exceed available seats. The district's IB programs are open to students district-wide through application. For families relocating to San Diego, SDUSD's neighborhood school assignment and magnet application processes are separate: families must verify their assigned school based on home address and separately apply for magnet or choice programs during the designated enrollment period. Confirm both processes directly on the SDUSD website before signing a lease or closing on a home, as school boundaries are defined by address and are subject to periodic redistricting.

planningTips: Military families transferring mid-year: Contact SDUSD's Military Family Liaison before your move date. The district is experienced with expedited enrollment, and families who reach out in advance are generally able to complete school placement within the first week of arrival. Unofficial records are typically accepted while official transcripts are in transit.

Cross-border families: SDUSD's Spring Recess (Mar 29–Apr 2) and Mexican schools' spring break (typically mid-April) do not overlap. If coordinating visits to family in Tijuana or Baja, plan separately for each school system.

San Diego's mild climate makes late-July and early-August back-to-school prep comfortable, but popular uniform and supply retailers near school zones sell out of common sizes by the first week of August. Shop by July 28.

Families new to California: Cesar Chavez Day (March 31) is a California school holiday not observed in most other states. In years when it falls outside spring recess, students will be off on a day that appears to be a normal school day on a non-California calendar.

SDUSD schedules multiple Minimum Days throughout the year — students dismiss 2–3 hours earlier than usual — for teacher professional development, parent conferences, and assessment periods. These dates are published on the SDUSD calendar at the start of each school year and require advance childcare planning, particularly for elementary school families.

Santa Ana wind events and wildfire smoke arrive in San Diego primarily from late September through November, overlapping with the start of the school year. On high-AQI days, SDUSD may cancel outdoor activities including recess and PE. Monitor the San Diego Air Quality Management District for daily AQI alerts during fall.

districtFaqs.q: Does San Diego Unified have special support for military families?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. SDUSD serves one of the largest concentrations of military families of any urban school district in California, given the proximity of Camp Pendleton, NAS North Island, MCAS Miramar, and Naval Base San Diego. The district has a Military Family Liaison to assist with mid-year transfers, expedited enrollment, and school placement. Military families who contact the district before their PCS move date are generally able to complete enrollment within the first week of arrival. Unofficial records are typically accepted while official transcripts are in transit.

districtFaqs.q: How does SDUSD's calendar affect families with ties to Tijuana or Baja California?
districtFaqs.a: Mexican public schools follow a September-to-June calendar with a spring break typically in mid-April — approximately two weeks later than SDUSD's spring recess (March 29–April 2). Cross-border families should check both calendars separately each year, as the two systems do not share a common spring break window.

districtFaqs.q: Is San Diego Unified the same as San Diego State University (SDSU)?
districtFaqs.a: No. San Diego Unified School District (SDUSD) is the K-12 public school system serving the city of San Diego. San Diego State University (SDSU) is a separate public university with its own semester academic calendar. SDSU's schedule has no effect on SDUSD school dates.

districtFaqs.q: Why are school closures due to weather so rare in San Diego Unified?
districtFaqs.a: San Diego's mild Mediterranean climate means extreme weather events that commonly cancel school elsewhere — heavy snow, ice storms, hurricanes, or severe thunderstorms — rarely affect the city. In most years, SDUSD completes the school year exactly as scheduled with no weather-related cancellations. Families relocating from the East Coast, Midwest, or Texas should note that the concept of a 'snow day' essentially does not exist in San Diego public schools.

districtFaqs.q: Do charter schools in San Diego follow the SDUSD calendar?
districtFaqs.a: Not necessarily. Independent charter schools that operate within San Diego Unified's boundaries set their own academic calendars and are not required to match SDUSD's school year. Some affiliated charters align closely with SDUSD, but many — especially those with national networks — use different start dates, break schedules, and instructional day counts. Always confirm directly with your charter school rather than assuming the SDUSD calendar applies.

districtFaqs.q: Does SDUSD offer dual language or bilingual programs?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. San Diego Unified offers Dual Language Immersion programs in Spanish and Mandarin at select elementary and middle school campuses, reflecting the district's large Spanish-speaking and Chinese-American student communities. Enrollment in these programs is through a separate application process and is not guaranteed by neighborhood attendance zone. Families interested in Dual Language Immersion should contact the district's school choice office for the current program list, school locations, and application deadlines.

districtFaqs.q: What is a Minimum Day in San Diego Unified?
districtFaqs.a: A Minimum Day is a shortened school day in which students are dismissed 2–3 hours earlier than on a regular school day. SDUSD schedules multiple Minimum Days throughout the year for teacher professional development, parent-teacher conferences, and state assessment administration. These dates are published at the start of each school year on the official SDUSD calendar. Elementary school families in particular should plan childcare in advance, as Minimum Days can fall on dates that appear to be regular school days without checking the calendar.

districtFaqs.q: How does wildfire smoke affect SDUSD school schedules?
districtFaqs.a: San Diego County experiences elevated wildfire risk from late September through November, when Santa Ana wind conditions can push smoke and particulates from inland fires into coastal communities. When the Air Quality Index (AQI) exceeds safe thresholds, SDUSD may cancel or modify outdoor activities — including recess and physical education — though school closures for air quality alone are uncommon. Families should monitor the San Diego Air Quality Management District (SDAPCD) for daily AQI forecasts during fall, particularly in years with active fires in San Diego, Riverside, or San Bernardino counties.

livingHere.intro: San Diego is home to more active-duty military personnel than any other city in the United States, and sits 15 miles from the US–Mexico border at Tijuana. These two facts set San Diego apart from every other large California school district and shape the rhythms of daily life for a significant share of SDUSD families.

highlights.label: Military Installations
highlights.detail: Camp Pendleton, Naval Air Station North Island, MCAS Miramar, and Naval Base San Diego collectively employ tens of thousands of military and civilian personnel in the metro area. Mid-year family relocations (PCS orders) make San Diego Unified one of the highest mid-year transfer districts in California — the district's Military Family Liaison is specifically staffed for this.

highlights.label: US–Mexico Border Proximity
highlights.detail: The San Ysidro Port of Entry — 15 miles from downtown San Diego — is one of the world's busiest land border crossings. Northbound wait times can reach 60–90 minutes or more during morning school commute hours, particularly on Fridays and Mondays. Families who regularly cross should factor border wait times into school arrival planning.

highlights.label: Tijuana Cross-Border Families
highlights.detail: A significant share of SDUSD students reside in Tijuana and cross daily for school. Mexican school calendars use a different spring break schedule — typically mid-April versus SDUSD's late March — meaning cross-border families must coordinate two separate academic calendars simultaneously.

highlights.label: Biotech, Defense, and Research Economy
highlights.detail: San Diego is consistently ranked among the top biotech clusters in the country, with major employers including Qualcomm, Illumina, and dozens of defense contractors concentrated near Miramar and Chula Vista. The Salk Institute, UC San Diego, and Scripps Research anchor the region's life sciences economy — driving strong STEM program demand and dual enrollment opportunities in SDUSD high schools.

highlights.label: Wildfire Smoke and Air Quality
highlights.detail: Santa Ana wind events from late September through November can push smoke and particulate matter from inland wildfires into San Diego's coastal communities. On elevated-AQI days, SDUSD may cancel outdoor activities at school — including recess and PE — though school closures for air quality alone are uncommon. Families relocating from the East Coast or Pacific Northwest should be aware that the back-to-school season coincides with the start of Southern California's wildfire risk window.

highlights.label: San Diego International Airport (SAN)
highlights.detail: Located 3 miles from downtown, SAN is one of the few major US airports inside a dense urban core. It serves domestic routes and select international destinations. During Spring Recess (late March) and Memorial Day weekend following the late-May school end, flights and rideshare wait times spike sharply — book and plan accordingly.

relatedDistricts.name: Los Angeles Unified School District
relatedDistricts.reason: The largest school district in California and the second largest in the United States. Families relocating along the Southern California coast between Los Angeles and San Diego frequently compare both districts' calendars and school programs.

relatedDistricts.name: Long Beach Unified School District
relatedDistricts.reason: A large Southern California district between Los Angeles and San Diego, and a common reference point for families comparing California coastal districts. Frequently compared by families in transit between San Diego and the LA metro area.
